answer:
**Puṇya** (merit) does not appear as a direct term in the English translations of the Smṛti corpus (e.g., *Manusmṛti*, *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, *Aṅgirasa Smṛti*, *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, etc.).[^1] However, the closely associated concept of "merit" (the standard English rendering of *puṇya*) is frequently described across these texts as the spiritual reward or auspicious fruit gained from righteous actions (*dharma*), rituals (e.g., *śrāddha*, sacrifices, gifts), filial duties like adopting or supporting sons, protecting deposits/refugees, truthful witness, and judicial fairness.[^2][^3]

Key themes from the available passages:
- **From rituals and gifts**: Planting trees (esp. *panasa*), performing *śrāddha* at auspicious times, or giving gifts yields "imperishable merit," "meritorious worlds," or multiplied rewards (e.g., sixfold for Brāhmaṇas, thousandfold for learned ones).[^4][^5][^6]
- **Filial and social duties**: Certain appointed sons (*vṛttidatta*, *vivāhadatta*) confer escalating "meritorious" benefits to ancestors; protecting trusts equals the merit of giving gold.[^7][^8]
- **Judicial and ethical acts**: Judges/assessors gain merit from fair enforcement; false witness destroys all lifetime merit.[^9][^10]
- **Sacrificial contexts**: The ritual patron (*yajamāna*) reaps the merit, even via agents.[^11]

This merit ensures salvation for ancestors, fulfillment, heavenly worlds, or inexhaustible fruits even gods cannot fully describe. No single definitional passage exists in the searched texts (*Manusmṛti* Chs. 1–12 and others yielded no "merit" hits), and not all 37 works cover it explicitly—the corpus emphasizes practical accrual via *dharma* over abstract theory. For deeper context, see *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* (extensive on *śrāddha* merit) and *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* (legal/gift merit).[^12]

[^1]: Corpus-wide searches for "punya", "\"punya\"", "puṇya", "\"puṇya\"", and "punya merit" returned zero results; "merit" yielded ~20, mostly *Aṅgirasa*/*Bṛhaspati*.

[^2]: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* 1, [p-d27356](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-d27356).

[^3]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* 1, [p-ba7477](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-ba7477).

[^4]: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* 1, [p-9f29f0](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-9f29f0).

[^5]: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* 1, [p-bd2f38](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-bd2f38).

[^6]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* 1, [p-ba7477](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-ba7477).

[^7]: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* 1, [p-d27356](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-d27356); [p-cbd9b0](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-cbd9b0).

[^8]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* 1, [p-8b3e8b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-8b3e8b).

[^9]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* 1, [p-90762a](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-90762a); [p-45b640](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-45b640).

[^10]: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* 1, [p-f977f9](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-f977f9).

[^11]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* 1, [p-97a468](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-97a468).

[^12]: Full chapters: *Aṅgirasa Smṛti* Ch. 1 [here](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/); *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* Ch. 1 [here](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/). *Manusmṛti* and *Viṣṇu Smṛti* (100 chapters) lack indexed "merit" mentions.
